name: GPT-4 and GPT-4o Essay Sample Augmentation for AI Scoring
population_or_context: Student essays across seven writing prompts, with subgroup and score-level analyses.
sample_size: Student essay samples ranged from 1,372 to 2,167 across prompts; generated data included about 120 GPT-4 essays per score level and about 275 GPT-4o essays per score level after filtering.
methods: GPT-4 and GPT-4o generated essays by prompt and score level using sampled student examples; DeBERTaV3 scoring models were trained on student-only and mixed student/synthetic samples at multiple student-sample fractions.
education_context: Writing assessment essays scored on a six-point scale.
response_origin: Hybrid: student-written essays plus GPT-4 or GPT-4o generated essays.
label_source: Human scores for student essays; generated essays were prompted for predetermined score levels and used in training.
generation_method: Prompted essay generation by score level and prompt, conditioned on sampled student essays receiving the same score.
prompt_strategy: Templates asked the model to generate essays matching prompt, score level, and randomly sampled same-score student examples.
evaluation_method: Compared text features, human-AI QWK/correlation/SMD on held-out student essays, subgroup MDSS/QWK fairness measures, and model predictions on generated essays.
scorer_use: Training augmentation, scorer performance benchmarking, and subgroup fairness analysis.
real_response_comparator: Yes. Models were evaluated on held-out student essays with human scores and compared against student-only training baselines.
sparsity_or_imbalance_motivation: The paper notes that certain subgroups and top/bottom score levels can have small samples; score categories 1, 5, and 6 had few student essays.
synthetic_data_research_role: Generated essays increased training sample size across prompts and score levels to examine scoring accuracy and fairness effects.
reported_metrics: QWK, correlation, standardized mean difference, subgroup MDSS, subgroup QWK; augmented models generally performed comparably to student-only models and mitigated some subgroup discrepancies.
limitations: Only one human rating was available; generated essays showed subtle content and style differences that require qualitative review.
